one_liner: A 29-year-old woman presents with 5 days of progressive blurred vision in her left eye with pain on eye movement, following an episode of right leg weakness and numbness 8 months ago that resolved over 6 weeks.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient noticed blurred vision in her left eye 5 days ago that has worsened progressively. She describes a dull aching pain behind the left eye, worse with eye movements. Colors appear 'washed out' when viewed with the left eye alone compared to the right. Eight months ago, she experienced numbness and weakness in her right leg that developed over 3 days, made walking difficult for 2 weeks, and gradually resolved over 6 weeks without treatment. She attributed it to a 'pinched nerve' and did not seek care. She also recalls an episode of electric shock-like sensations running down her spine when flexing her neck (Lhermitte sign) about 4 months ago that lasted a few weeks. She reports increased fatigue for the past year and occasional urinary urgency. No recent infections or vaccinations.
stage2.medications: Combined oral contraceptive pills. Ibuprofen PRN.
stage2.family_history: Maternal aunt diagnosed with multiple sclerosis at age 35. No other autoimmune diseases.
stage2.social_history: Marketing analyst. Non-smoker. Social alcohol (1-2 drinks/week). No illicit drug use. Grew up in Minnesota (northern latitude). No recent travel.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Well-appearing young woman in no acute distress. HEENT: Left eye — visual acuity 20/80 (right eye 20/20). Left relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) on swinging flashlight test. Pain with left eye movement. Red desaturation in the left eye. Fundoscopy: mild left optic disc edema. Right eye normal. Neuro: Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Motor: 5/5 strength in upper extremities bilaterally. Right hip flexion and ankle dorsiflexion 4+/5. Left lower extremity 5/5. Sensory: Decreased vibration sense in right foot. Lhermitte sign positive on neck flexion. DTRs: 3+ at right knee and ankle (brisk) with upgoing right plantar response (Babinski sign). Left side 2+ with downgoing plantar. Coordination: Mild intention tremor on left finger-to-nose testing. Gait: Mild right foot circumduction.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal; helps exclude systemic inflammatory/vasculitic conditions
Negative; reduces likelihood of SLE-related CNS disease
Negative; helps differentiate MS from neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder

lab_results.result: 5 unique bands in CSF, absent in serum
lab_results.clinical_significance: Positive oligoclonal bands support intrathecal IgG synthesis, found in >85% of MS patients
Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis typical of MS; rules out infection
Elevated IgG index supports intrathecal immunoglobulin production consistent with MS

imaging_results.test_name: MRI brain with and without contrast
imaging_results.result: Multiple ovoid T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in the periventricular white matter (perpendicular to ventricles — Dawson fingers pattern), juxtacortical regions, and left optic nerve. One periventricular lesion shows gadolinium enhancement. At least 9 T2 lesions total.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Periventricular lesions with Dawson fingers morphology are highly characteristic of MS. Gadolinium-enhancing lesion indicates active demyelination. Meets dissemination in space criteria.

imaging_results.result: Single T2 hyperintense lesion at C3-C4 level, non-enhancing, spanning less than 2 vertebral segments.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Short-segment spinal cord lesion typical of MS (as opposed to longitudinally extensive lesion of NMOSD). Explains prior right leg symptoms and Lhermitte sign.

key_tests: MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast
CSF analysis with oligoclonal bands and IgG index
Visual evoked potentials (if subclinical optic nerve involvement suspected)
low_yield_tests: CT head (poor sensitivity for white matter lesions)
stage3.low_yield_tests_note: CT head is insufficient for evaluating demyelinating lesions — MRI is the imaging modality of choice.

stage5.final_clue: The combination of two clinical attacks (optic neuritis now and myelitis 8 months ago) involving different CNS locations (optic nerve and spinal cord), supported by MRI showing dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord lesions) and dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions), along with positive CSF oligoclonal bands, confirms the diagnosis of this condition this condition per the 2017 McDonald criteria. The patient receives IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days for the acute optic neuritis, with improvement in vision over the following weeks. She is counseled about disease-modifying therapies and started on a high-efficacy DMT.

debrief.classic_presentation: Young woman (20-40 years) with relapsing neurological deficits separated in time and space: optic neuritis (eye pain, vision loss, RAPD), myelitis (limb weakness/numbness, Lhermitte sign, Babinski sign), with MRI showing periventricular Dawson fingers lesions and positive CSF oligoclonal bands.
debrief.pathophysiology: MS is an autoimmune, inflammatory demyelinating disease of the CNS in which autoreactive T cells cross the blood-brain barrier and, with B cells and macrophages, attack myelin sheaths and oligodendrocytes. Acute lesions show perivenular inflammation and demyelination, causing conduction block and neurological deficits. Remyelination can occur (explaining relapse recovery), but repeated injury leads to axonal loss and progressive disability. The periventricular predilection reflects the perivenular distribution of lesions around deep medullary veins. Cortical and grey matter involvement contributes to cognitive decline.
debrief.treatment_overview: Acute relapse: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day x 3-5 days (shortens relapse duration but does not alter long-term disability). Plasma exchange for steroid-refractory relapses. Disease-modifying therapy (DMT): High-efficacy therapies (ocrelizumab, natalizumab, ofatumumab, alemtuzumab) vs. moderate-efficacy (dimethyl fumarate, fingolimod, teriflunomide, glatiramer acetate, interferons). Current trend favors early high-efficacy therapy to prevent disability accrual. Symptomatic: Baclofen/tizanidine for spasticity, amantadine for fatigue, anticholinergics for bladder urgency. Physical therapy, occupational therapy, cognitive rehabilitation.
high_yield_pearls: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow MS diagnosis with a single clinical attack IF MRI shows dissemination in space AND time — CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time.
Optic neuritis: eye pain worse with movement + vision loss + RAPD (Marcus Gunn pupil) is the classic triad. It is the presenting symptom of MS in ~20% of cases.
Lhermitte sign (electric shock sensation down the spine with neck flexion) indicates posterior column demyelination in the cervical spinal cord — classic for MS but not pathognomonic.
Dawson fingers are periventricular lesions oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricles on sagittal FLAIR MRI — they follow perivenular inflammation and are highly characteristic of MS.

NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibody) must be tested to exclude NMOSD, which requires different treatment — NMOSD favors longitudinally extensive spinal lesions (>=3 vertebral segments) and severe bilateral optic neuritis.
sources: 2017 McDonald Criteria for the Diagnosis of Multiple Sclerosis. Lancet Neurology, 2018
AAN Practice Guideline: Disease-Modifying Therapies for MS, 2018
UpToDate: Manifestations of multiple sclerosis in adults
